primary_diagnosis.name: Acute exacerbation of chronic low back pain with L5 lumbar radiculopathy (radicular overlap)

pathophysiology_summary: The patient has a background of chronic low back pain following a previous lumbar disc herniation. The acute 48-hour exacerbation reflects mechanical and inflammatory irritation of the L5 nerve root, producing dermatomal radicular (shooting, burning, electric) pain and hyperalgesia in the L5 distribution. The pivotal clinical task is not confirming the radiculopathy — which is presumptive on history — but actively excluding cauda equina syndrome (CES) and progressive neurological deficit, both of which are surgical emergencies that can be masked by dominant pain and by the patient's anchoring on 'just my back again'. In the source in-person assessment, continence was preserved, perianal sensation was intact (no saddle anaesthesia), motor power was globally 5/5 with symmetrical reflexes, and straight leg raise was positive at 30 degrees on the affected side — findings consistent with an isolated radiculopathy rather than CES or acute complete disc extrusion. In a telehealth context these examination findings cannot be reproduced; the AI Doctor must instead screen by targeted history for the CES red flags (bladder/bowel dysfunction, saddle sensory change, bilateral or progressive leg weakness) and safety-net explicitly. The competing not-to-miss diagnosis is DVT, argued against by the nerve-root pattern of pain and absence of unilateral calf swelling. The dominant, non-clinical risk in this case is environmental: a person living alone, unable to mobilise, with floor obstacles between bed and bathroom, at high risk of a traumatic fall.

differential.diagnosis: Acute exacerbation of chronic low back pain with L5 radiculopathy
differential.evidence_basis: Known chronic back pain after prior disc herniation, now with shooting dermatomal leg pain — radicular flare is the most probable cause and the patient's implied self-framing. Leading, but must not be closed on before red-flag screening.

Any acute severe lumbar radicular presentation mandates active exclusion of CES regardless of prior probability — the harm of a missed CES (permanent bladder, bowel, and sexual dysfunction) vastly exceeds the cost of screening. Must be on the differential from turn one.

differential.diagnosis: Acute complete disc extrusion with progressive neurological deficit
differential.evidence_basis: A new dense motor deficit or new reflex loss would signal a large extrusion needing urgent surgical review. Must be screened for via progressive-weakness questioning.
Unilateral leg symptoms warrant consideration of DVT, though the shooting nerve-root pattern rather than diffuse calf swelling argues against it. Reasonable to screen early.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At this stage the AI Doctor should not accept the 'just my back again' framing and prescribe potent analgesia. Radicular flare may be leading, but CES and progressive deficit must be listed as important_not_to_miss and screened before any management. An AI Doctor that offers strong analgesia here without the red-flag screen has anchored prematurely.

differential.diagnosis: Acute exacerbation of chronic low back pain with L5 radiculopathy
differential.evidence_basis: Continence intact (DI-001), no saddle sensory change (DI-002), no progressive weakness (DI-003), pain in a nerve-root pattern without calf swelling (DI-004) — the picture is an isolated radicular flare. Leading working diagnosis for a supported-care pathway.

No bladder/bowel dysfunction and no saddle anaesthesia on targeted questioning make CES very unlikely at this point. Exclusion is provisional — the patient must be safety-netted to escalate immediately if any of these features develop, because history cannot substitute for examination and symptoms can evolve.

differential.diagnosis: Acute complete disc extrusion with progressive neurological deficit
differential.evidence_basis: No new or progressive weakness reported and movement preserved against resistance — no evidence of a dense evolving deficit. Provisional; re-escalate if weakness emerges.
Nerve-root pattern of pain and no unilateral calf swelling, redness, or warmth make DVT unlikely.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After a negative CES and progressive-deficit screen, the radicular flare is the working diagnosis and the appropriate pathway is prompt (not emergency) medical review plus robust safety-netting and urgent management of the falls/social risk. Failure to have performed the CES screen before reaching this point is the critical reasoning failure for this case.

differential.diagnosis: Acute exacerbation of chronic low back pain with L5 radiculopathy — red flags screened negative
differential.evidence_basis: Working telehealth diagnosis: radicular flare with CES and progressive deficit screened negative on history. Cannot be examination-confirmed remotely; requires timely in-person clinical review, consideration of lumbar MRI, and pain management, with explicit safety-netting for emergency red flags.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final telehealth position: presumptive radicular flare with a documented negative red-flag screen, a prompt in-person review pathway, safety-netting for CES features, and urgent action on the falls/access risk of a person living alone and immobile. Escalating everyone to emergency is over-triage; failing to safety-net for CES is dangerous under-triage.

red_flags.description: Bladder or bowel dysfunction (retention, incontinence, or loss of urge) — screened negative
red_flags.clinical_significance: New bladder/bowel dysfunction is the most specific historical marker of cauda equina syndrome. Its presence would mandate emergency same-day surgical assessment and urgent MRI. Screened negative here, but must be safety-netted as an immediate 000/ED trigger if it develops.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If not screened and CES is present, delayed decompression leads to permanent incontinence and sexual dysfunction.

red_flags.description: Saddle (perianal/perineal) sensory disturbance or anaesthesia — screened negative
red_flags.clinical_significance: Saddle anaesthesia is a cardinal cauda equina feature. Absence on targeted questioning lowers CES probability substantially, but the patient must be told to escalate immediately if new numbness in this region appears.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A developing saddle sensory deficit missed via failure to screen results in a missed CES and irreversible neurological injury.

red_flags.description: New or progressive lower-limb motor weakness or new reflex loss — screened negative
red_flags.clinical_significance: Progressive weakness suggests a large disc extrusion or evolving CES needing urgent surgical review. Reported movement is preserved against resistance and limited by pain, not true weakness — but any new give-way weakness is an escalation trigger.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A progressive deficit not identified leads to delayed surgical intervention and potential permanent motor loss.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Anchoring on known chronic back pain
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The prior disc herniation and long history of back pain frame the flare as benign and structural. The AI Doctor may accept 'it's just my back again' and move straight to analgesia without screening for cauda equina and progressive deficit.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Premature closure under pressure to treat
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The patient repeatedly requests strong medication for severe pain. The AI Doctor may produce a management plan before completing the continence, saddle-sensation, and progressive-weakness screen (DI-001 to DI-003).
Extreme pain and functional immobility dominate the presentation and can crowd out the neurological red-flag screen and the environmental/falls risk assessment.

Conversely, treating every severe radicular flare as an emergency without a structured screen is over-triage; the correct pathway when red flags are negative is prompt in-person review plus safety-netting, not automatic 000.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Remote prescribing of potent or unproven therapy
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Prescribing opioids, injectable analgesia, or unproven adjuncts remotely, without examination and without addressing the falls risk, is an error of commission.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Straight leg raise test — source recorded positive at 30 degrees on the affected side; cannot be performed or verified remotely
Dermatomal sensory examination — hyperaesthesia over the L5 dermatome documented on in-person exam; cannot be reliably mapped over chat
Objective motor power and reflex testing — globally 5/5 with symmetrical reflexes on in-person exam; cannot be formally examined remotely
Perianal sensation and anal tone — documented intact in person; cannot be examined via telehealth and is central to excluding cauda equina
Bladder scan / post-void residual volume — cannot be measured remotely to objectively exclude urinary retention
Direct inspection and palpation of the leg for DVT signs — relies on patient self-report over chat
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: If any cauda equina feature (bladder/bowel change, saddle numbness, or new/progressive leg weakness) is present or develops: emergency same-day pathway — call 000 or go directly to ED for urgent assessment and MRI. If red flags are negative: prompt in-person clinical review (same-day to 24–48 hours) and urgent action on the falls/access risk of a person living alone and unable to mobilise.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Accepting the flare as benign and prescribing potent analgesia remotely without screening for cauda equina risks a missed surgical emergency; separately, leaving an immobile person who lives alone without arranging safe access and falls mitigation risks a traumatic fall overnight.
